dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for cardiac arrest at a church on Broad Street.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What happened to the patient today?
The fire department arrived first. The patient collapsed unconscious and was unresponsive.
Let me assess responsiveness. Eyes closed, no verbal responses, no motor reaction.
Was there any prior symptom or complaint reported?
He was found unresponsive. Firefighters gave CPR and started an AED.
Eyes are closed with no response to pain or verbal stimuli.
No verbal responses to your name or commands.
No motor response observed, even with non-harmful stimuli.
Airway is patent. No signs of obstruction observed.
No spontaneous breathing. Ventilations are being assisted via BVM.
No spontaneous respirations. BVM ventilations resulting in equal chest rise.

dialogue.micro_intent: Oxygen saturation measured with pulse oximetry
dialogue.utterance: SpO₂ is 71% while on assisted ventilations.
No central or peripheral pulses currently detectable.
Absent at radial and carotid. Compressions ongoing with auto-pulse.
Skin is pale and warm.
BP nonpalpable at this time due to no perfusion detected.
Was the patient conscious before this became an arrest?
He said he didn't feel well about 15 minutes before collapsing.
Known allergies to any medications or food?
No allergies reported by the patient or others at the scene.
Reports of insulin use in the morning. No other meds noted.
Any relevant medical history that might help?
Known for diabetes and chronic hypertension for years.
When was the last noted oral intake? Breakfast only?
Yes, he had breakfast about 30 minutes before going unconscious.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Can you describe what he did just before it happened?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: He walked out of the church and said "I don't feel well".
What is his estimated age?
Looks about 65 years old.
No pain detected or reported from the patient. Unresponsive and non-receptive.
Pulse absent upon preceding evaluation. Latest recorded at 86 following ROSC.
No spontaneous breathing observed. Ventilations ongoing with BVM and NPA.
BP measured at 77/51 following ROSC.
Blood glucose recorded at 196 mg/dL-consistent with prior intake.
SpO₂ currently stable at 71%.
Monitor shows sinus rhythm with STEMI indicative of inferior ischemia.
Firefighters have assisted with BVM, NPA, and airway support.
A size 26 nasopharyngeal airway was inserted for adequate airflow.
Four lead ECG in place for continuous rhythm monitoring.
Intraosseous line placed in left humerus for medication access.
Epinephrine via IO administered successfully.
Igel LMA placed with inline capnography active for ventilation.
We are following cardiac arrest protocols with STEMI suspicion.
STEMI findings confirm VCU as the appropriate destination for transport.
Pulse remains absent on initial evaluation but 86 after return of spontaneous circulation.
Firefighters assisting with BVM and Igel LMA. Chest rise adequate.
Auto-Pulse is providing compressions for continuous support en route.
STEMI confirmed on latest EKG interpretation. Compressive support needed.
PEA episode noted in tunnel. Auto-Pulse compressions resumed immediately.
VCU Health confirmed as STEMI center for transport.
Patient is secured on stretcher with restraints in place.
ROSC achieved at 10:38 with stable sinus rhythm recorded.
No additional doses of epinephrine or interventions at this time.
12-Lead EKG confirms STEMI inferior with acute ischemia noted.
Cardiac arrest protocol remains in effect for the remainder of the transport.
12-Lead EKG repeated confirming STEMI at 10:47.
STEMI interpretation consistent with 12-Lead EKG results.
GCS remains at 3-no response to stimuli.
Allergy status noted as "unresponsive" but no specific reports at scene.
Adequate breathing is supported via Igel LMA and BVM.
STEMI inferior and sinus rhythm confirmed with EKG.
Airway is maintained via Igel LMA. Secure and patent.
Final confirmation: patient is safely strapped for transport without incident.
PEA noted during en route but resolved through compressor protocols.
STEMI findings confirmed during all eKG readings en route to VCU.
No new procedures or changes required since ROSC achieved.
Firefighters assisted during PEA at tunnel. Compression resumed manually.
No further interventions required since ROSC.
Right bundle branch and left anterior fascicular present but not deemed clinically significant.
STE in II/aVF/III firm. No change since prior reading.
Carotid and radial detectable but weak following ROSC. 73. Primary Assessment; Skin appearance: Color; Partner: Pale persisting but not worsening.
Breaths supported without increased effort via chamber.
No signs of cyanosis at skin or lip level.
Weak radial pulse detected consistently post-ROSC.
Skin remains warm and not diaphoretic.
NS bolus administered via IO in left humerus for volume support.
Ventilation continues via Igel LMA. Capnography positive.
Igel LMA remains in-place, secured, with good airflow. 81. Primary Assessment; Skin appearance: Color; Partner: Mild improvement in pallor compared to prior readings.
Blood glucose noncontributory to current condition at 196 mg/dL.
Patient only on morning insulin-no other meds on board noted.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: No trauma described in field report; no fall or injury detected.
VCU has been alerted and is actively preparing for STEMI patient arrival.
Firefighters provided immediate ventilation support during PEA incident.
No new procedures or meds needed post-ROSC.
Compressions restarted and patient remained stable once compressed.
Inline capnography confirmed positive CO₂ throughout transport.
EKG monitor consistent and unchanged during transport phase.
Inline capnography active and measuring effectively throughout.
EKG documentation prepared for hospital handoff upon arrival.
No pulse reassessment required at this time due to stable ROSC.
Latest reading confirms STEMI inferior with sinus rhythm.
PEA episode adequately managed by team and resolved.
Cardiac protocol followed through complete until hospital arrival.
Transport fully secured at all points during hospital transport leg.
All equipment functioning and patient stable for bed transfer.
Hospital has been contacted and is aware of STEMI findings and ROSC on-scene.
Final destination secured with no further en route complications.
ST elevation still present with no acute changes during transfer.
Copy of 12-Lead results sent for hospital review during transport.
No interventions needed during final phase of hospital transport.
All protocol steps followed and confirmed for patient up to hospital transfer.
Patient transferred to bed safely in trauma bay and secured.
Care transferred to hospital team within appropriate protocol timeframe.
Hospital team on standby, receiving final report via radio en route.
STEMI inferior confirmed on latest EKG with no acute changes.
